data.title: Energy shocks drive European EV sales 51% higher in March
data.description:
Electric vehicle adoption across Europe is accelerating sharply as the ongoing Iran conflict disrupts global energy markets and pushes fuel prices higher. 

Battery electric vehicle ((BEV)) registrations soared 51% Y/Y in key European markets in March 2026, with more than 224,000 units sold in the month alone, data from New Automotive and trade association E-Mobility Europe showed.

“March’s surge in electric car sales is one of Europe’s biggest recent gains in energy security, in a month when oil dependence has become a real vulnerability. Across the EU’s major markets, EV sales are growing at rates above 40%, marking a clear step change, not statistical noise. That translates into half a million electric cars registered so far this year, cutting roughly two million barrels of oil demand annually.” said Chris Heron, Secretary-General of E-Mobility Europe.

Region-wise, Italy is picking up pace, with BEV registrations rising 65% YTD, lifting market share from around 5% at the end of 2025 to 8.6% in March. Germany is rebounding after new incentives, with about one in four cars sold in March fully electric and BEV sales up 42% YTD.

France remains the leader among large markets, with a 28% BEV share in March, supported by leasing schemes and nearly 50% growth YTD. The Nordic region continues to lead EV adoption, with Denmark at 76.6% fully electric sales in March and Finland close to 50%, both showing strong growth. Norway remains the global benchmark, with 98.4% of new car sales in March being fully electric.

"At a time when energy security has moved to the top of the political agenda, the EV transition is delivering real and measurable resilience. The pace of change we’re now seeing across major European markets—including countries like Italy and Poland that were slower to start—suggests the transition has entered a new phase." said Ben Nelmes, CEO of New Automotive.

Automaker tickers: Volkswagen AG (VWAGY) (VWAPY), Mercedes-Benz Group AG (MBGAF), BMW AG (BMWKY), Porsche AG (POAHY), Renault SA (RNSDF), Stellantis N.V. (STLA), Ferrari N.V. (RACE), Volvo Car AB (VOLAF), Polestar Automotive (PSNY), Aston Martin Lagonda (AMGDF), Tesla (TSLA), Ford Motor (F), General Motors (GM), BYD Company (BYDDF) (BYDDY), NIO (NIO), XPeng (XPEV), Geely Automobile (GELYF), SAIC Motor (SAIC), Leapmotor (ZGJLY), Kia (KIMTF), Nissan Motor (NSANY), Toyota Motor (TM).

Citi services wins five-year custody and fund services mandate extension from Hostplus
data.description:
• Citi Services Tuesday announced it has won a mandate to provide custodial and fund services to superannuation fund Hostplus in a five-year contract extension.
  • Citi (C) was the incumbent party in a competitive tender process. The retention of the mandate builds on Citi's existing 13-year partnership with Hostplus.
  • Hostplus is one of the largest superannuation funds in Australia, with over 1.9 million members, more than 320,000 employers, and over $150 billion in funds under management.
  • Over their 13-year partnership, Citi has worked closely with Hostplus as it has scaled and grown in complexity; supporting their increased investment requirements, regulatory obligations, and operational efficiency.

data.title: JPMorgan extends $1.5T security and resilience initiative to Europe
data.description:
JPMorgan (JPM) Tuesday announced the expansion of its $1.5T, 10-year Security and Resiliency Initiative across Europe.

“The national and economic security of countries depends on strong, resilient and reliable supply chains, and robust critical industries,” said Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO of JPMorganChase.

“For too long, the U.S. and Europe have relied on unpredictable sources for things like critical minerals that are essential to collective security and prosperity. Now, it is in our best interest to address these challenges together—because our security, freedom and economic growth depend on it.”

To support the European expansion, the firm is investing in talent to facilitate SRI’s activation across the five key verticals, including supply chain and advanced manufacturing, defense and aerospace, energy independence and resilience, frontier and strategic technologies, and pharma and healthtech.

In response to growing interest from clients and in close partnership with Global Banking’s Head of SRI, Jay Horine, CEOs for Europe, the Middle East and Africa (EMEA) — Conor Hillery and Matthieu Wiltz — will provide leadership, oversight and accountability for SRI in the region. 

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) also announced its intention to appoint Admiral Sir Tony Radakin, the former Chief of the U.K. Defence Staff, to the SRI External Advisory Council.

“The collective experience of our External Advisory Council is a real force multiplier for SRI and will help support our strategic financing of critical industries to deliver meaningful impact in an increasingly complex global environment,” said Dimon.

data.title: Tuesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Retail Sales

Retail sales measure the total receipts at stores that sell merchandise and related services to final consumers. Sales are by retail and food services stores. 

Recall these are not inflation-adjusted numbers, so rising gas prices translate into higher sales, expected to be up 1.4 percent on the month, with support from a rebound in auto sales. Ex-vehicles and gas, the consensus sees a modest rise of 0.2 percent.

10:00 AM Business Inventories 

Business inventories are the dollar amount of inventories held by manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers. 

A moderate 0.2 percent increase for February is expected after a 0.1 percent decrease in January.

10:00 AM Pending Home Sales Index

The National Association of Realtors developed the pending home sales index as a leading indicator of housing activity.

The consensus sees sales up 0.9 percent in March, building on the 1.8 percent rise in February.

10:00 AM Senate Banking Committee Nomination Hearing of Kevin Warsh

Senate Banking Committee holds a hearing on the nomination of Kevin Warsh to be the next chair of the Federal Reserve.

2:30 PM Christopher Waller Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Christopher Waller speaks on "Modernizing Reserve Bank Operations" before the Brookings Institution.

data.title: Bezos targets $38B valuation as Project Prometheus nears $10B funding milestone: FT
data.description:
• Jeff Bezos is close to finalizing a $10B funding round for his AI startup that’s developing models with the capability of understanding the physical world, the Financial Times reported.
  • The fresh funding values the company, code-named Project Prometheus, at $38B, the newspaper said, citing people familiar with the deal. JPMorgan (JPM) and BlackRock (BLK) are among the investors in the new round, the FT said.
  • The round is expected to close soon but has not been finalized, according to the report.
  • Project Prometheus, the artificial intelligence company created by Amazon (AMZN) co-founder Jeff Bezos, earlier this month hired xAI (X.AI) co-founder Kyle Kozic away from OpenAI (OPENAI).

data.title: Trump invokes Cold War-era Defense Production Act to fund new energy projects
data.description:
President Donald Trump invoked the Defense ​Production Act to sign ‌a series of presidential memorandums providing federal funds for a wide range of energy projects, the White House announced Monday, as his administration faces pressure to help curb rising oil, gasoline, and electricity costs.

Trump signed five memorandums under the law, targeting coal supply chains, ​domestic petroleum production, natural gas transmission and ​liquefied natural gas capacity, and power grid infrastructure - areas where the president said insufficiencies threaten national defense.

Projects eligible for support could include coal-fired power plants, refineries and facilities that manufacture gas turbines and transformers - equipment that has been subject to shortages.

The move allows the Department of Energy to deploy funding that was secured last year in Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill tax-and-spending package.

The Defense Production ​Act is ​a ⁠Cold War-era piece of legislation aimed at boosting production of goods ​for national security purposes.

On Monday, crude oil futures recovered more than half of Friday's losses as uncertainty over the prospect of peace talks between the U.S. and Iran and the status of the Strait of Hormuz re-ignited supply fears as the end of a two-week ceasefire approaches.

"The sudden sense of optimism that overwhelmed markets on Friday has given way to threats and accusations," Mizuho's Robert Yawger said in a note. "Meanwhile, the Strait of Hormuz remains shut, and every day the strategic choke point remains closed brings the world one day closer to an energy crisis."

Iranian state media cast doubt on the likelihood of a second round of talks, saying the U.S. must lift its blockade on Iranian ports; after-hours, crude oil fell following reports indicating that Iran would send a team to Pakistan for talks.

Oil prices could rise to $110/bbl if traffic in the Strait of Hormuz remains disrupted for another month, Citigroup analysts said.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery rallied 6.9% to $89.61/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery jumped 5.6% to $95.48/bbl, the largest one-day gain for both benchmarks since April 2.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) edged higher for a fourth straight session, helped by a slight cooling in temperature outlooks; the front-month Nymex May contract settled up 0.6% at $2.689/MMBtu.
